Client: I think it’s around 160 a year? Maybe a little more.
Advisor: Okay—around 160k gross. When you say “a little more,” are we talking like 162-ish, 170-ish…?
Client: Closer to 163. I have it written down as $162,800 gross for the year.
Advisor: Perfect, $162,800 gross annually. And that’s just you—single household, right?
Client: Yes, just me.

Client: Around five thousand a month.
Advisor: Okay, about $5,000. Is that pretty steady, or are we missing big items like insurance premiums or property stuff?
Client: Right—sorry, it’s more like $5,550 a month when I include everything.
Advisor: Great, $5,550 per month total expenses. I’m just writing that down—one sec.
Advisor: Now debts—do you have any monthly debt payments? Mortgage, car loan, credit cards you’re carrying?

Client: Sounds good.
Advisor: First, going back to the big picture—what’s your gross household income these days, roughly, Margaret? Like before taxes.
Client: I think it’s around 160 a year? Maybe a little more.
Advisor: Okay—around 160k gross. When you say “a little more,” are we talking like 162-ish, 170-ish…?
Client: Closer to 163. I have it written down as $162,800 gross for the year.

Advisor: Great. Let’s shift to assets. Roughly how much do you have in investable assets—so not counting the home value—across taxable, retirement, and anything private?
Client: Around nine hundred thousand.
Advisor: Okay, around $900k. Do you know the more exact total you’re using?
Client: Yes—$899,200 is what I have as investable assets total.
Advisor: Perfect—$899,200 investable assets.

Advisor: Great—$57,300 private markets via brokerage.
Advisor: And then your home—just so we have the full net worth picture—what do you estimate the primary residence value at?
Client: About $879,800.
Advisor: Okay, $879,800 for the primary residence value.
Advisor: And no mortgage against it, right?

Advisor: Let’s switch gears from debt to the bigger picture—risk and taxes—then we’ll do a quick sweep of income, assets, and your disability coverage so nothing’s missing. Sound okay?
Client: Yeah, that’s fine.
Advisor: First, on investing style—when markets drop, do you generally want to stay aggressive, or do you want more of a cushion?
Client: I’m pretty aggressive. I can tolerate the swings if there’s a plan.
Advisor: Aggressive as in you’re okay with volatility for growth—got it.

Advisor: And just so I’m not assuming—aggressive doesn’t mean reckless. It just means higher stock exposure, less smoothing. That still feel like you?
Client: Yeah—exactly.
Advisor: Great. Next one’s a little “RIA jargon,” sorry—are we working together in a U.S. RIA setup, like a US-based registered investment advisor relationship?
Client: Uh, I think so? I mean you’re a U.S. RIA, right?
Advisor: Yes—US RIA. I just wanted to confirm that’s the market context we’re in for how accounts are held and billed.

Client: Okay, then yes—US RIA.
Advisor: Perfect. Now taxes—do you have a sense of what bracket you’re landing in these days? Like low 20s, 30s…
Client: I’m in the 35% bracket.
Advisor: 35%—thank you.
Advisor: Quick check: when you say 35%, you mean your marginal federal bracket, not your effective rate, right?

Client: Sounds good.
Advisor: First, going back to the big picture—what’s your gross household income these days, roughly, Margaret? Like before taxes.
Client: I think it’s around 160 a year? Maybe a little more.
Advisor: Okay—around 160k gross. When you say “a little more,” are we talking like 162-ish, 170-ish…?

Advisor: Got it—marginal 35%.
Advisor: Okay. Now, household basics—since you’re divorced, is it just you in the household, one adult?
Client: Yes, just me—one adult.
Advisor: Thank you. I know that’s a weirdly blunt question.

Client: Yes, divorced.
Advisor: Got it. And where are you filing from—what state are you a resident of?
Client: Florida.
Advisor: Florida—okay. And country-wise, you’re here in the U.S.?
Client: Yes, U.S.

Advisor: Hi Margaret—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what made you reach out now?
Client: Hi Alex. Post-divorce, honestly. I just want to make sure I’m on track and not missing something big.
Advisor: Totally get it. We’ll keep it practical. I’ll ask a few basics first—income, spending, assets, and any debts—then we can circle back to retirement timing and taxes.
Client: Sounds good.

Client: I think it’s around 160 a year? Maybe a little more.

Client: Yeah—sounds good.
Advisor: Okay—sounds good. Going back to what you said earlier about keeping taxes simple, we’ll stay high level.
Advisor: Margaret, can I just confirm your work situation right now—are you currently employed, or are you fully retired at this point?
Client: I’m employed, yeah. Still working.
Advisor: Got it—still employed. And the gross pay from work, all-in for the year, is that about $162,800?

Advisor: Okay, that helps. Sorry—quick micro-clarifier: when you say “gross,” you mean before taxes and benefits, not what lands in your checking account.
Client: Right, before taxes. Net is lower.
Advisor: Great. Now, occupation-wise—how would you describe what you do? Like, are you in a professional role—manager, analyst, attorney, engineer—something along those lines?
Client: Yeah, professional. Office job, not hourly or anything.
Advisor: Okay, professional category—thank you.

Client: Sounds good.

Client: Uh, I always mix up whether people want month-day-year or—

Client: Wait—sorry, did you say “1966”?

Advisor: Got it—$63,500 annualized rental income, monthly.
Advisor: And investment income—interest and dividends—do you see that regularly or more lumpy?
Client: Quarterly. Around $19,900 a year, give or take.
Advisor: Okay, quarterly interest and dividends, about $19,900 annualized.
Advisor: Quick check—when you say “gross income,” you’re not talking about what hits your checking account after withholding, right?

Client: Yeah, professional. Office job, not hourly or anything.
Advisor: Okay, professional category—thank you.
Advisor: One small admin thing, Margaret—sometimes we label people in the household as Client 1, Client 2, etc. Since it’s just you, can I confirm how you want that labeled? Are you okay being “Client 1” on our side?
Client: Yes, I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And just to be super clear, it’s a single-adult household—just you, right?
